food_subgroup: Vegetarian curry with paneer and vegetable dumplings in cream-based gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Shahi Malai Kofta. Mapped to closest_parent curry food product class (FOODON_03311069). A more specific term for paneer/vegetable kofta curry does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetable dishes - cooked vegetable dishes with sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Shahi Malai Kofta. Mapped to A0CKP (cooked vegetable dishes with sauce) as the closest defensible parent category. The dish contains paneer (cottage cheese) dumplings and vegetables in a cream-almond gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Shahi Malai Kofta as a specific food entity. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

main_ingredients: spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Shahi Malai Kofta is approximately 300 g, comprising 4–6 paneer and vegetable dumplings submerged in a generous portion of creamy almond-cashew gravy, usually accompanied by naan or rice ordered separately.

nutrient_source.title: Malai Kofta / Paneer Kofta Curry — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component entries: Paneer (per 100 g), Potato (boiled), Cream, Almond, and Vegetable curry with gravy
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Shahi Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using ICMR-NIN 2017 tables for paneer, boiled potato, cream, almond paste, tomato-onion gravy base, and cooking oil/ghee. Approximate ingredient proportions per 100 g finished dish: paneer 20 g, potato/vegetable 15 g, cream 10 g, almond-cashew paste 8 g, tomato-onion-spice gravy 40 g, oil/ghee 7 g. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for Palak Paneer and similar North Indian cream curries as a plausibility anchor.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry with paneer and cream-based gravy — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable curry (GI ~45–50) and paneer-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shahi Malai Kofta in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry (GI ~45–55, low-medium range); (2) the high fat and protein content of this dish (cream, paneer, almond paste) which substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response; (3) the relatively low carbohydrate content (~9.2 g/100 g) dominated by potato and vegetable starch partially offset by fat and protein. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 27.6) / 100 = 12.4. Serving carbohydrate: 9.2 g/100 g × 300 g = 27.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Shahi Malai Kofta to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 300 g restaurant portion delivers approximately 930 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~12.4 per serving) and high saturated fat from cream and paneer
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Shahi Malai Kofta in controlled portions (150–200 g) given its low GI (~45) driven by high fat and protein content, but should account for the moderate glycemic load and elevated saturated fat, and pair it with a low-GI staple such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti rather than refined naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.9 g/100 g; ~17.7 g per serving) from cream, paneer, and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should treat Shahi Malai Kofta as an occasional dish, as a 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 17.7 g of saturated fat — nearly 90% of the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit — and should request preparation with reduced cream and ghee where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shahi Malai Kofta is a luxurious North Indian vegetarian curry in which soft dumplings (koftas) made from paneer (fresh cottage cheese) blended with mashed potato, mixed vegetables, and spices are deep-fried until golden, then simmered in a velvety, mildly spiced gravy of cream (malai), almond paste, cashew paste, tomato, and onion. The word 'Shahi' means 'royal' in Hindi and Urdu, reflecting the dish's Mughal-era origins as a banquet preparation for the imperial court. The sauce is characteristically ivory to pale orange in colour, rich, and mildly sweet from the nut pastes, with a gentle warmth from cardamom, cinnamon, and white pepper rather than the sharp heat of chilli-forward curries. The koftas are tender inside with a slightly crisp exterior that softens once submerged in the gravy. Nutritionally, Shahi Malai Kofta is energy-dense (~168 kcal/100 g) with a high fat content (~12.4 g/100 g) dominated by saturated fat from cream and paneer, moderate protein (~5.8 g/100 g) from paneer, and relatively low carbohydrate (~9.2 g/100 g), yielding a low glycemic index (~45) that makes portion-controlled servings manageable for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a good source of calcium (~112 mg/100 g) from paneer and cream. Sodium is moderately high in restaurant preparations (~310 mg/100 g), warranting caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. In Singapore, Shahi Malai Kofta is served at North Indian restaurants in the Boat Quay and South Bridge Road corridor and is typically ordered as part of a shared meal with naan, roti, or basmati rice.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Shahi Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries for paneer, cream, almond, potato, and vegetable curry base using weighted ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar cream-based vegetable curries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-characterised and the ICMR-NIN source is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 component tables; no single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Shahi Malai Kofta as a finished dish. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source tables for the component ingredients used in this estimation. 3. Selenium is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 tables for the relevant component ingredients. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~45) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry with cream and nut paste; no direct GI measurement for Shahi Malai Kofta was found. 5. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 6. The food_name has been normalised from 'Shahi Malai Kofta (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical 'Shahi Malai Kofta'; the promotional qualifier 'Chef Recommendation' is captured in the aliases. 7. Halal status is flagged true as the dish is vegetarian and contains no pork or alcohol; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. FoodOn match type changed from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. 9. FoodEx2 match type changed from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. 10. SNOMED CT null with gap note; no codes invented.
